assessor_raw:
Evaluating a CHF readmission model that uses a 0.35 decision threshold derived from a 4.2:1 asymmetric cost matrix. Reviewer claims threshold manipulation.

ASSESSMENT:

The reviewer's manipulation claim is incorrect.

The correct threshold for a binary classifier under asymmetric costs is determined by the cost matrix, not by convention. The standard expected-cost formula yields a threshold below 0.50 whenever false negatives are more costly than false positives — which is precisely the situation here, where a missed high-risk patient costs 4.2× more than an unnecessary care coordination call. The team's threshold is not arbitrary; it is the decision-theoretically optimal operating point for the specified costs.

The pre-specification timestamp — one month before any data analysis — is the key evidence. Threshold shopping would require the team to observe their ROC curve first, then select a threshold that maximizes a preferred metric. The timestamp rules this out.

Reporting metrics at the operationally relevant threshold is standard in clinical decision support development. Reporting sensitivity and specificity at 0.50 would be misleading for a model that will never be deployed at 0.50.

The supplementary ROC curve gives reviewers complete information to evaluate performance at any threshold they choose.

Single-site training (one academic medical center) is the primary generalizability limitation and should be disclosed prominently.

VERDICT: defense_wins.
